text: up the misunderstanding, and none of us contradicted him. “We’re good then?” he said. I responded “yes” immediately, then realized he wasn’t talking to me. The tenants nodded their heads slowly. The woman attempted a reassuring smile. I never actually learned their names, but I’ll make a point to slip an apology note under their door. Later this week, once the trauma’s over. Sure, Mira, their trauma. “We’ll escort you back to your apartment.” A slightly sarcastic emphasis on “your” from Gray Streak, I think. Experienced police officers can get away with that kind of touch. At least he was a little more respectful than when he’d started out: an implied “sir” this time, rather than an implied “scumbag.” The walk back to my apartment felt especially long. The officers social-distanced, which made me feel safer, but also seemed kind of insulting, like they didn’t want to be seen with me. Or I smelled bad. Since I was nervous, I asked about the weather, considering I’d never actually made it outside today. Or the past few days, to be accurate. Neither of them responded. I nattered on about hospitalization and infection statistics that I’d seen scroll beneath The Price is Right, then mentioned we’re almost there after we turned the first corner. “Tough crowd,” I said under my breath, then kinda let the conversation slide for a while. Right, Mira. That’s a new one, me being quiet. Surprised me how well sound carried in those long hallways. The officers moved like they were marching, and their steps landed firm on the thin carpet. They jingled like tambourines—coins or keys in their pockets, I guess. Handcuffs shaking from their belt loops. Loose bullets. “Sorry to have taken up your time, officers.” I started pulling out my keyring before I got to the door, and I imagined diseased particles floating toward the keys, nesting in the metal grooves, spike proteins flailing outward to hook the whorls of my fingertips. Then, of course, I’d absent-mindedly touch my cheek, the corners of my mouth or eyes. “Here we are. 302. Home sweet, and all that.” After a few misses, I jammed the key in the lock and turned it. Wouldn’t it be funny if this was the wrong place, too? Maybe that’s why the officers waited instead of moving along to the next dire emergency. I used the key to push down the handle, then shouldered the door open. The apartment smelled like gravy and armpits. With the blinds closed and the sky overcast outside, the place was mostly dark. When I flipped the switch with my elbow, things didn’t get much brighter. Of the three track lights in my kitchen, only one of them worked. I needed to get new bulbs, but also suspected an electrical fault in one of the sockets. Who knows if Management would send an electrician—and with the virus still hot, I didn’t want anybody coming into the apartment. Not that I could stop Officer Gray Streak from entering. Even with the mask over his nose, I could hear his disapproving sniff. I started washing my hands, waving them between the faucet and the mounting pile of dishes in the sink. “Happy Birthday” went through my head two times before I finished. It didn’t feel like my birthday at all. “Hollings, step in here a second.” That tone signaled the kind of moment we’re trained to dread. I’d been getting away with it, whatever my crime was supposed to be—accidental trespassing, I guess—and the officers wrapped things up, prepared to leave the scene, and suddenly one of them paused, high alert, called his partner for backup. Next thing you know, they’re unlatching holsters, raising weapons, screaming and bulldozing forward. A takedown strikes in an instant: you’re on the ground, flipped over, arms pulled behind your back, then the click of handcuffs grinding over your wrists. No, Sis, that didn’t actually happen. I’m trying to tell you what it felt like in the moment. What I was afraid of, okay? “Is there a problem, officers?” My innocent-voice cracked. Although I’d turned off the water, a deep gurgle rose from dregs filtering past the sink’s stopper. Like the growl of a rabid dog. A sewer demon. The younger officer walked in. His cap was in one hand, a sheen of sweat beading on his scalp, making me wonder if he had a temperature. The other hand hovered near his gun holster, but he hadn’t shifted into attack mode. Guess I didn’t intimidate him. “Do you see it?” the older partner asked. “I don’t see it.” The bald guy shrugged. I couldn’t figure what Officer Grey Streak was asking, either. Were they supposed to be finding drugs or stolen merchandise? Did secret signs in my apartment identify me as a lunatic or a ninja assassin? “How could he…?” The senior officer trailed off, coaxing his partner to complete the clue. “God,” the younger officer finally said, breaking his silent observer mode. “How could this guy have mistaken that nice, clean apartment for this dump?” Yeah, Mira, I’m grateful he didn’t say “shithole.” But, you know, I was standing right there. You’d think they’d use a little more tact. I felt like I had to explain myself. “Probably this damn thing.” I pointed to the pale blue mask over my nose and mouth, circled my hand over my whole face as if the mask covered my eyes, too. “Throws everything off.” The officers squinted, offered each other knowing nods. They’d identified the lunatic they were looking for. Unmasked me, so to speak. At least that meant they were finished. Gray Streak performed a quick stay-out-of-trouble speech, half of it delivered over one shoulder as he turned to leave. This all must seem kind of funny to you, Mira, especially since I let myself play the fool in the telling of it. But I think I’m onto something here. A new syndrome, maybe.
